one_liner: A 64-year-old man with poorly controlled type 2 diabetes presents with 3 weeks of worsening pain, swelling, and a non-healing ulcer on the sole of his right foot with purulent drainage.

stage2.hpi_extension: He first noticed a small callus on the plantar aspect of his right first metatarsal head four months ago. Two months ago it became an open ulcer. Over the past three weeks, the area has become increasingly red and swollen, with malodorous purulent discharge. He reports subjective fevers and fatigue but denies chills or rigors. He has had difficulty feeling his feet for years and often walks barefoot at home.
stage2.pmh: Type 2 diabetes for 15 years (HbA1c 10.2% last month), peripheral neuropathy, hypertension, hyperlipidemia
stage2.family_history: Father with diabetes and coronary disease
stage2.social_history: Retired mechanic. Former smoker (40 pack-years, quit 5 years ago). No IV drug use.
stage2.physical_exam: General: ill-appearing. Right foot: **2 x 3 cm deep plantar ulcer over the first metatarsal head** with surrounding erythema, warmth, and mild swelling extending onto the dorsum; malodorous purulent drainage. **A sterile metal probe passed through the ulcer reaches bone ('probe-to-bone' positive)**. Decreased sensation to monofilament testing bilaterally. Pedal pulses diminished but palpable. No crepitus. No proximal streaking.

lab_results.result: WBC 14.8 K/uL with 82% neutrophils. **ESR 96 mm/hr**. **CRP 14 mg/dL**. Glucose 312 mg/dL.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Markedly elevated **ESR and CRP** are sensitive (though nonspecific) markers for osteomyelitis. An ESR >70 mm/hr in a diabetic with a foot ulcer greatly increases the probability of underlying bone infection.

lab_results.result: Plain radiograph of the right foot: subtle cortical irregularity and periosteal reaction along the first metatarsal head; no definite lytic lesion. **MRI of the right foot: bone marrow edema, cortical destruction, and enhancement of the first metatarsal head consistent with osteomyelitis; small adjacent soft tissue abscess.**
lab_results.clinical_significance: **MRI is the most sensitive imaging modality** for osteomyelitis and detects marrow edema days to weeks before plain films show lytic changes. It also delineates soft tissue involvement to guide debridement.

lab_results.result: **Bone biopsy culture grows methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus (MSSA)**. Blood cultures x 2: negative.
lab_results.clinical_significance: **Bone biopsy with culture is the gold standard** for diagnosing osteomyelitis and identifying the causative organism. **Staphylococcus aureus is the most common cause overall**, including in diabetic foot osteomyelitis.

stage5.final_clue: He undergoes surgical debridement of necrotic bone and soft tissue and is started on a **6-week course of IV nafcillin** with tight glycemic control and diabetic foot care teaching. Serial CRP and ESR trend downward over the following weeks.

debrief.classic_presentation: Osteomyelitis presents with localized bone pain, erythema, warmth, swelling, fever, and sometimes a draining sinus tract. Mechanisms include **hematogenous seeding** (most common in children — long bone metaphysis; vertebral in adults), **contiguous spread** (diabetic foot ulcer, decubitus ulcer, postoperative wound, open fracture), and **direct inoculation** (trauma, surgery, prosthesis). In diabetic foot infections, a **'probe-to-bone' test** plus an ESR >70 mm/hr strongly supports underlying osteomyelitis.
debrief.pathophysiology: Bacteria reach bone via bloodstream, contiguous infection, or direct inoculation. Infection incites inflammation, increased intraosseous pressure, and vascular compromise that leads to **bone necrosis (sequestrum)** and reactive new bone formation (**involucrum**). Chronic osteomyelitis features walled-off abscesses (Brodie abscess) and sinus tracts. **Staphylococcus aureus** is the most common organism in all age groups; **Salmonella** is classic in **sickle cell disease**; **Pseudomonas aeruginosa** in **IV drug use and puncture wounds through a sneaker**; **Pasteurella** after cat/dog bites; **Mycobacterium tuberculosis** causes vertebral osteomyelitis (**Pott disease**).
debrief.treatment_overview: Treatment requires a combination of **prolonged (4-6 weeks) IV antibiotics** tailored to culture results and **surgical debridement** of necrotic bone and any abscess. Empiric therapy typically covers MSSA/MRSA (vancomycin) with gram-negative coverage (e.g., cefepime or piperacillin-tazobactam) when indicated. Optimization of glycemic control, offloading of diabetic foot ulcers, and revascularization when peripheral arterial disease coexists are essential. Chronic osteomyelitis may require staged surgery and long-term suppressive antibiotics.
high_yield_pearls: **Staphylococcus aureus** is the most common cause of osteomyelitis at all ages.
**Salmonella** osteomyelitis classically occurs in **sickle cell disease**; **Pseudomonas** in **IV drug users and puncture wounds through a sneaker**; **Pasteurella** after **cat/dog bites**.
**MRI is the most sensitive imaging modality** and detects marrow edema days to weeks before plain X-rays become abnormal.
**Bone biopsy with culture is the gold standard** for diagnosis and tailoring antibiotic therapy.
Treatment is **4-6 weeks of IV antibiotics plus surgical debridement** of necrotic bone; failure to remove sequestrum leads to chronic osteomyelitis.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Musculoskeletal, Skin, and Connective Tissue chapter, 'Osteomyelitis' section.
First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Musculoskeletal, Skin, and Connective Tissue chapter, 'Common bone infections and organisms' table.
